136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**

156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. 205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. 231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).

235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:

241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.

245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.

255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):

262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.

275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.

282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |

69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. 83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}

101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}

237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}

284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}

307	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
308	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
310	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
311	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
312	  % Polynomials" (read from
313	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
315	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
316	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
317	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
318	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
319	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
320	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
322	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
323	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
324	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
325	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
326	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
327	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
328	  such that for all \(n > N\)
329	  \[
330	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
331	  \]
332	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
333	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
334	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
335	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
336	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
337	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
338	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
339	
340	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
341	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
342	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
344	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
345	  rational series from the module.
346	\end{lemma}
347	
348	\begin{proof}
349	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
350	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
351	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
352	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
353	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
354	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
355	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
356	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
357	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
358	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
359	  graded modules
360	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
361	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
362	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
363	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
364	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
365	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
366	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
367	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
368	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
369	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
370	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
371	\end{proof}

373	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
374	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
376	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
377	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
378	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
379	  % Polynomials" (read from
380	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
381	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
382	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
383	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
384	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
385	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
386	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
387	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
388	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
389	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
390	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
391	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
392	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
393	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
394	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
395	  \[
396	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
397	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
398	  \]
399	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
400	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
401	\end{theorem}
402	
403	\begin{proof}
404	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
405	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
406	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
407	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
408	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
409	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
410	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
411	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
412	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
413	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
414	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
415	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
416	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
417	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
418	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
419	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
420	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
421	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
422	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
423	\end{proof}

425	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
426	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
427	
428	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
429	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
430	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
431	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
432	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
433	
434	\begin{definition}
435	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
436	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
437	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
438	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
439	  % NOTE: basic-open coherence depends on lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen
440	  % (section restriction as a localization for quasi-coherent sheaves on a general scheme),
441	  % which is not yet formally closed.
442	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
443	  % Polynomials" (read from
444	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
445	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
446	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
447	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
448	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
449	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
450	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
451	  project-built primitive.}
452	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
453	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
454	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
455	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
456	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
457	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
458	  \(U \subseteq X\),
459	  \[
460	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
461	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
462	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
463	  \]
464	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
465	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
466	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
467	
468	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
469	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
470	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
471	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
472	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
473	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
474	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
475	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
476	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
477	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
478	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
479	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
480	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
481	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
482	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
483	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
484	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
485	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
486	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
487	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
488	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
489	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
490	
491	  \emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
492	  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
493	\end{definition}
494	
495	\begin{lemma}\leanok
496	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
497	  localization]
498	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
499	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
500	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
501	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
502	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
503	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
504	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
505	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
506	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
507	  \[
508	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
509	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
510	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
511	  \]
512	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
513	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
514	
515	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
516	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
517	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
518	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
519	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
520	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
521	\end{lemma}
522	
523	\begin{proof}
524	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
525	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
526	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
527	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
528	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
529	
530	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
531	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
532	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
533	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
534	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
535	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
536	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
537	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
538	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
539	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
540	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
541	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
542	\end{proof}

644	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]

import Mathlib

/-!
# The Quot scheme: Quot-foundations layer

This file packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman Quot-scheme construction. It introduces the
per-fiber Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf, the Quot functor
`Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `T`-flat coherent quotients, and the Grassmannian
*scheme* (Mathlib at the pinned commit carries only a linear-algebra
Grassmannian) together with its representability.

The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` (def) — the
   **Hilbert polynomial function** `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` of a coherent
   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a
   line bundle `L`. Encoded as a function `S → Polynomial ℚ`.

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` (def) — the **Quot
   functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` sending an
   `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the set of equivalence classes
   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on
   `X_T`, `q : E_T ↠ F` a surjection, and `F|_{X_t}` having Hilbert
   polynomial `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` (def) — the
   **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
   rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V`.

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` (theorem)
   — the **representability of the Grassmannian** by a smooth projective
   `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with
   the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, not
  `Unit`; the Hilbert polynomial is a non-trivial invariant of the
  coherent sheaf at the fiber over `s`.
- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return contravariant functors into
  `Type u` — substantive presheaves of sets, not constant functors.
- `Grassmannian.representable` packages the
  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure: existence of a
  scheme `Y` together with a `RepresentableBy Y` witness — substantive

241	/-- **Locally free of rank `d`** for a sheaf of modules on a scheme.
242	
243	A sheaf of modules `M` on a scheme `X` is *locally free of rank `d`* when `X`
244	admits an open cover `{U i}` on each member of which the restriction
245	`M|_{U i}` (the pullback of `M` along the open immersion `(U i).ι`) is
246	isomorphic to the free module of rank `d`, `O_{U i}^{⊕ d}` (encoded as
247	`SheafOfModules.free (ULift (Fin d))` over the structure-ring sheaf of the
248	open subscheme `(U i).toScheme`).

250	This predicate is project-local: Mathlib does not supply a rank-indexed local
251	freeness predicate for sheaves of modules on a scheme. Blueprint:
252	`def:is_locally_free_of_rank` (Nitsure §1, Exercise (2)). -/
253	def IsLocallyFreeOfRank {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) (d : ℕ) : Prop :=
254	  ∃ (ι : Type u) (U : ι → X.Opens), (⨆ i, U i = ⊤) ∧
255	    ∀ i, Nonempty ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (U i).ι).obj M ≅
256	      _root_.SheafOfModules.free (R := (U i).toScheme.ringCatSheaf) (ULift.{u} (Fin d)))

section IsQuasicoherent

open Limits

/-- Tilde of `R` as an `R`-module is isomorphic to the structure sheaf `𝒪_{Spec R}`. -/
noncomputable
def tildeSelf : tilde (ModuleCat.of R R) ≅ SheafOfModules.unit.{u} _ := .refl _

instance : IsIso (Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ (SheafOfModules.unit.{u} (Spec R).ringCatSheaf)) :=
  isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff.mpr ⟨_, ⟨tildeSelf⟩⟩

/-- Tilde of direct sums of `R` as an `R`-module is isomorphic to the free sheaf. -/
noncomputable
def tildeFinsupp (ι : Type u) : tilde (ModuleCat.of R (ι →₀ R)) ≅ SheafOfModules.free.{u} ι :=
  letI H : IsColimit <| (tilde.functor R).mapCocone (ModuleCat.finsuppCocone R R ι) :=
    isColimitOfPreserves (tilde.functor R) (ModuleCat.finsuppCoconeIsColimit R R ι)
  letI iso : (Discrete.functor fun (_ : ι) ↦ ModuleCat.of R R) ⋙ tilde.functor R ≅
         Discrete.functor fun _ ↦ SheafOfModules.unit.{u} _ :=
      Discrete.natIso (fun _ ↦ tildeSelf)
  IsColimit.coconePointUniqueUpToIso
    ((IsColimit.precomposeHomEquiv iso.symm _).symm H) (coproductIsCoproduct _)

instance (ι : Type u) :
    IsIso (Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ (R := R) (SheafOfModules.free.{u} ι)) :=
  isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff.mpr ⟨_, ⟨tildeFinsupp _⟩⟩

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/-- Given a presentation of a module `M`, we may construct an associated presentation of `M^~`. -/
noncomputable
def presentationTilde (s : Set M) (hs : Submodule.span R s = ⊤)
    (t : Set (s →₀ R))
    (ht : Submodule.span R t = LinearMap.ker (Finsupp.linearCombination R ((↑) : s → M))) :
    (tilde M).Presentation := by
  haveI H₁ : Function.Exact
      (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination (α := t) R (↑)))
      (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination (α := s) (M := M) R (↑))) :=
    (LinearMap.exact_iff.mpr (by simp [Finsupp.range_linearCombination, ht]))
  refine SheafOfModules.presentationOfIsCokernelFree.{u}
      ((tildeFinsupp t).inv ≫ tilde.map (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination R (↑))) ≫
        (tildeFinsupp s).hom) ((tildeFinsupp s).inv ≫
          tilde.map (ModuleCat.ofHom (Finsupp.linearCombination R (↑)))) (by
    simp only [Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_assoc, Preadditive.IsIso.comp_left_eq_zero]
    rw [← tilde.map_comp, ← ModuleCat.ofHom_comp]
    convert tilde.map_zero
    exact congr(ModuleCat.ofHom $(H₁.linearMap_comp_eq_zero))) ?_
  letI h₁ := ModuleCat.isColimitCokernelCofork _ _ H₁
    (by simp [← LinearMap.range_eq_top, Finsupp.range_linearCombination, hs])
  refine IsCokernel.ofIso _ (CokernelCofork.mapIsColimit _ h₁ (tilde.functor R)) _ (tildeFinsupp t)
    (tildeFinsupp s) (.refl _) (by simp) (by simp)

instance : (tilde M).IsQuasicoherent :=
  (presentationTilde.{u} _ .univ (by simp) _ (Submodule.span_eq _)).isQuasicoherent

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
lemma isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation (M : (Spec R).Modules) (P : M.Presentation) :
    IsIso M.fromTildeΓ := by
  rw [isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff]
  let g := (tilde.functor _).preimage <| (tildeFinsupp _).hom ≫ P.relations.π ≫ kernel.ι _ ≫
    (tildeFinsupp _).inv
  let iso : cokernel ((tilde.functor R).map g) ≅ cokernel (P.relations.π ≫ kernel.ι _) := by
    refine cokernel.mapIso _ _ (tildeFinsupp _) (tildeFinsupp _) ?_
    simp only [g, (tilde.functor R).map_preimage]
    simp
  exact ⟨cokernel g, ⟨PreservesCokernel.iso (tilde.functor R) g ≪≫ iso ≪≫
    IsColimit.coconePointUniqueUpToIso (colimit.isColimit _) P.isColimit⟩⟩

end IsQuasicoherent

theorem existsUnique_hilbertPoly (p : F[X]) (d : ℕ) :
    ∃! h : F[X], ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n > N,
      (p * invOneSubPow F d : F⟦X⟧).coeff n = h.eval (n : F) := by
  use hilbertPoly p d; constructor
  · use p.natDegree
    exact fun n => coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d
  · rintro h ⟨N, hhN⟩
    apply eq_of_infinite_eval_eq h (hilbertPoly p d)
    apply ((Set.Ioi_infinite (max N p.natDegree)).image cast_injective.injOn).mono
    rintro x ⟨n, hn, rfl⟩
    simp only [Set.mem_Ioi, sup_lt_iff, Set.mem_setOf_eq] at hn ⊢
    rw [← coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d hn.2, hhN n hn.1]

49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.

75

77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>

83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```

106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash

126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.

13865	\begin{proof}
13866	Let $f(n) - f(n-1) = \sum\nolimits_{i = 0}^r \binom{n}{i} a_i$
13867	for all $n \gg 0$. Set
13868	$g(n) = f(n) - \sum\nolimits_{i = 0}^r \binom{n + 1}{i + 1} a_i$.
13869	Then $g(n) - g(n-1) = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$. Hence $g$ is
13870	eventually constant, say equal to $a_{-1}$. We leave it
13871	to the reader to show that
13872	$a_{-1} + \sum\nolimits_{i = 0}^r \binom{n + 1}{i + 1} a_i$
13873	has the required shape (see remark above the lemma).
13874	\end{proof}
13875	
13876	\begin{lemma}
13877	\label{lemma-graded-module-fg}
13878	If $M$ is a finitely generated graded $S$-module,
13879	and if $S$ is finitely generated over $S_0$, then
13880	each $M_n$ is a finite $S_0$-module.
13881	\end{lemma}
13882	
13883	\begin{proof}
13884	Suppose the generators of $M$ are $m_i$ and the generators
13885	of $S$ are $f_i$. By taking homogeneous components we may
13886	assume that the $m_i$ and the $f_i$ are homogeneous
13887	and we may assume $f_i \in S_{+}$. In this case it is
13888	clear that each $M_n$ is generated over $S_0$
13889	by the ``monomials'' $\prod f_i^{e_i} m_j$ whose
13890	degree is $n$.
13891	\end{proof}
13892	
13893	\begin{proposition}
13894	\label{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial}
13895	Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
13896	and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
13897	function
13898	$$
13899	\mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
13900	n \longmapsto [M_n]
13901	$$
13902	see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
13903	If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
13904	then this function is a numerical polynomial.
13905	\end{proposition}
13906	
13907	\begin{proof}
13908	We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
13909	generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
13910	$M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
13911	To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
13912	be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
13913	the induction hypothesis applies to the
13914	graded ring $S/(x)$.
13915	
13916	\medskip\noindent
13917	First we show the result holds if $x$ is nilpotent on $M$.
13918	This we do by induction on the minimal integer $r$ such that
13919	$x^r M  = 0$. If $r = 1$, then $M$ is a module over $S/xS$
13920	and the result holds (by the other induction hypothesis).
13921	If $r > 1$, then we can find a short exact sequence
13922	$0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0$ such that the integers
13923	$r', r''$ are strictly smaller than $r$. Thus we know
13924	the result for $M''$ and $M'$. Hence
13925	we get the result for $M$ because of the relation
13926	$
13927	[M_d]  = [M'_d] + [M''_d]
13928	$
13929	in $K'_0(S_0)$.
13930	
13931	\medskip\noindent
13932	If $x$ is not nilpotent on $M$, let $M' \subset M$ be
13933	the largest submodule on which $x$ is nilpotent.
13934	Consider the exact sequence $0 \to M' \to M \to M/M' \to 0$
13935	we see again it suffices to prove the result for $M/M'$. In other
13936	words we may assume that multiplication by $x$ is injective.
13937	
13938	\medskip\noindent
13939	Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
13940	is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
13941	not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
13942	following short exact sequence
13943	$$
13944	0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
13945	$$
13946	This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
13947	Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}.
13948	\end{proof}
13949	
13950	\begin{remark}
13951	\label{remark-period-polynomial}
13952	If $S$ is still Noetherian but $S$ is not generated in degree $1$,
13953	then the function associated to a graded $S$-module is a periodic
13954	polynomial (i.e., it is a numerical polynomial on the
13955	congruence classes of integers modulo $n$ for some $n$).

559	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
560	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
562	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
563	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
564	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
565	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
566	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
567	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
568	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
569	  %  associated to $M$.
570	  %  [...]
571	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
572	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
573	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
574	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
575	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on \(X\)
576	  that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type} (over a locally noetherian
577	  base, matching the coherence encoding under which \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) is a
578	  finite \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module on each affine open). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
579	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
580	  \begin{enumerate}
581	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
582	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
583	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
584	    \item the section-restriction map
585	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
586	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
587	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
588	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
589	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
590	  \end{enumerate}
591	\end{lemma}
592	
593	\begin{proof}
594	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
595	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
596	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
597	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
598	
599	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
600	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf of finite type is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
601	  finite \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
602	  \(N\). The Spec-local computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
603	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
604	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
605	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
606	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
607	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
608	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
609	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
610	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
611	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
612	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
613	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
614	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
615	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
616	\end{proof}

377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
379	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
380	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
381	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
382	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
383	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
384	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
385	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
386	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
387	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
388	  graded modules
389	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
390	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
391	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
392	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
393	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
394	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
395	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
